audit_trail.notes: Three reviewers completed two full cycles. The HTML source rendered numerous mathematical symbols as dashes or blank labels, which limited algebraic auditability of key equations (particularly Eqs. 9–17) across all reviewers. All three reviewers independently converged on the same two core objections (no local field equation for α(R); circular/inverse fitting procedure) without prompting. Cycle 2 produced minor score adjustments and one additional explicit major objection from Claude (unjustified Friedmann-to-local-dynamics transfer, adopting a point raised by Codex in cycle 1), while Codex slightly reduced its overall score from 3.8 to 3.6 after confirming convergence. Gemini's cycle-2 score (3.0) remained stable. The paper is self-described as a proof-of-concept; this framing is noted but does not resolve the identified methodological issues.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes that an alternative cosmological model — one in which the fundamental constants of nature slowly change as the universe expands — can also explain why galaxies rotate faster at their edges than their visible matter alone would predict, without invoking invisible "dark matter." The review board found the core idea speculative but not obviously wrong. However, the paper's main test is compromised: the baryonic rotation curve that the model claims to predict is largely reverse-engineered from the very observational data it is compared against, which strips the comparison of independent evidential weight. The most important unresolved gap is that the paper provides no physical law governing how the model's central parameter should vary inside a galaxy.
human_spotchecks: Verify the PDF or LaTeX source of Equations (9)–(17): the HTML rendering drops key mathematical symbols throughout (shown as dashes or blank labels), making it impossible to confirm the sign and algebraic form of the α-matter term in Equation (13), the exact mapping of X(R) in Equations (15)–(17), and the dimensional consistency of all density terms.

Inspect prior CCC papers (particularly refs 61–62, 73–75) to determine whether a local field equation or post-Newtonian weak-field limit is derived that governs the spatial variation of α inside virialized systems. If such a derivation exists, the 'no local field equation' objection — the most consequential concern — would be at least partially mitigated.

Assess whether the informational content of V_bX is genuinely independent of V_o after tracing the full numerical chain of Equations (15)–(17). Specifically: how many effective degrees of freedom does the reconstruction have beyond the one declared turn-off density parameter, and can agreement with SPARC baryonic curves fail in principle?

Verify the supernova light-curve time dilation treatment in Gupta (2024, ref 60): the paper defers resolution of the tired-light time-dilation tension entirely to prior work. A quantitative check of whether that resolution is adequate or merely argued as accommodatable would affect the viability of the entire CCC+TL framework imported here.

Check whether the seven SPARC rotation-curve fits remain visually comparable under disk-geometry rather than spherical mass inversion, and under realistic SPARC observational uncertainties (distance, inclination, stellar mass-to-light) — the paper provides no sensitivity analysis for these systematic effects.
public_summary:
Gupta (2025) argues that dark matter is not a physical substance but an artifact of how fundamental coupling constants (G, c, ℏ, k_B) vary with cosmic expansion. Within the author's CCC+TL framework, the dimensionless parameter α generates α-matter and α-energy terms in modified Friedmann equations; locally, these terms are claimed to provide the extra gravitational pull attributed to dark matter. Applied to seven SPARC galaxies using a spherical approximation and a single free parameter per galaxy (turn-off density), the paper aims to show that predicted baryonic velocity curves visually resemble the baryonic estimates in the SPARC database. The paper is explicitly framed as a proof-of-concept.

All three reviewers (claude, codex, gemini) across two review cycles assigned low technical soundness scores (2.0–3.2/10) and converged on the diagnosis that the methodology does not constitute a genuine predictive test of the framework. The two objections that survived all cycles without dispute are the absence of any local dynamical law for α(R) and the circular structure of the fitting procedure. A third objection — that applying FLRW-derived Friedmann equations directly to local galactic dynamics conflates the cosmological metric with a local gravitational potential — was rated fatal by one reviewer and major by the other two; whether it is ultimately fatal depends on whether prior CCC papers establish a valid weak-field limit, which is a human spot-check item. The paper additionally lacks chi-squared or residual analysis, propagates no observational uncertainties, and uses spherical geometry for disk-dominated galaxies without quantifying the resulting error. A brief extension to galaxy clusters (Figure 12) is illustrative only and not evidence for the model.
reviewer_disagreements: Verdict severity: Gemini recommends 'major_flaw' and rates the FLRW-to-local-dynamics transfer as a 'fatal' flaw, while Claude and Codex recommend 'weak_evidence' and rate the same issue as 'major,' arguing it could be partially mitigated if prior CCC papers (refs 61–62, 73–75) establish a valid post-Newtonian or local weak-field limit — a question flagged for human spot-check.

Significance and novelty scores diverge: Codex assigns significance 5.4–5.8 and novelty 6.1–6.4 (reflecting the importance of the rotation-curve problem and the novelty of applying CCC+TL to galaxies), while Claude gives 3.0 and 5.0 and Gemini gives 4.0 and 5.0, with Claude explicitly arguing the methodological circularity prevents genuine progress on the problem and that novelty should not be inflated for a failed test.

Tired light applicability: Claude raises the embedding of H_e = 0.82 H_0 (the expansion-only Hubble constant) in local galactic density normalizations as a moderate concern, introducing an ~18% systematic offset without physical justification at galactic scales. Codex explicitly disputes this, arguing the TL component is not central to the local rotation-curve fits as presented and that photon tired-light losses inside galaxies are negligible.

Mass-to-light ratio sourcing: Claude identifies the use of private email communication with McGaugh as the primary citation for disk and bulge mass-to-light ratios as a reproducibility concern. Codex argues this is overstated because the paper also cites Schombert et al. (2022) and the values resemble commonly used SPARC fiducials.

strongest_objections.objection: The local radial variation of α (or equivalently X(R)) inside a galaxy is asserted to arise from local baryonic anisotropy but is not derived from any governing field equation, Lagrangian, screening law, or boundary-value problem. Without such a law, the model does not make predictions for galaxy dynamics — it provides a flexible per-galaxy reconstruction ansatz.

The rotation-curve test is substantially circular: X(R) and the density profile ρ_o(R) are extracted by numerical differentiation of the observed velocity V_o via Equations (15)–(17), and the baryonic curve V_bX is then reconstructed by integrating the same inferred density. Comparing V_bX against SPARC baryonic estimates is not an independent prediction — both curves trace the same observational data through different mathematical transformations.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 3, Equations (15)–(17); paragraph beginning 'We wish to emphasize that our method…'

strongest_objections.objection: The α-matter and α-energy terms are derived from the modified Friedmann equations, which assume a homogeneous, isotropic FLRW metric. Using these cosmological-expansion source terms as direct gravitational contributions in a Keplerian enclosed-mass relation (Equation 15) for a virialized, strongly anisotropic disk galaxy requires an explicit derivation of the CCC post-Newtonian or thin-disk limit — which is absent. One reviewer rates this fatal; two rate it major pending inspection of prior CCC papers.

No quantitative goodness-of-fit metric (χ², residuals, confidence intervals, or comparison baselines against ΛCDM+NFW or MOND) is reported. All fits are evaluated by visual inspection and qualitative labels ('Good fit', 'OK overall fit'), making it impossible to judge whether CCC+TL outperforms, matches, or underperforms standard alternatives on the same galaxies.

The spherical enclosed-mass relation M(<R) = V²R/G is applied to morphologically disk-dominated galaxies throughout, introducing systematic errors of order unity in inferred density profiles and rotation velocities. The paper acknowledges this as a limitation but treats it as a minor correction rather than a source of unquantified systematic bias affecting all quantitative results.

strongest_supported_claims: The modified Friedmann equations in the CCC+TL framework can be algebraically rearranged to expose terms that formally parallel dark matter and dark energy contributions (labeled α-matter and α-energy), and this algebraic structure is internally self-consistent within the paper's notation.

The α-matter-to-baryon density ratio decreases with increasing redshift in the CCC+TL model (from ~8 at z=0 to ~1.2 at z=7.31 per Figure 2), which is qualitatively consistent with the observed tendency for high-redshift galaxies to show more Keplerian (declining) rotation curves.
For flat outer rotation curves under the spherical approximation, the paper claims the implied baryonic density scales as R^{-4}, formally analogous to Hernquist-profile behavior (Equations 18–19).

Across the seven-galaxy sample in Table 1, the turn-off density varies by approximately 4× while the turn-off radius varies by approximately 8×, supporting the paper's claim that turn-off density is a more stable per-galaxy parameter than turn-off radius, though the sample is small and hand-selected.
technical_takeaway: The paper extends the CCC+TL framework to galaxy rotation curves by showing that modified Friedmann equations contain α-matter and α-energy terms that can substitute for dark matter when α is allowed to vary locally with baryonic density. All three reviewers across two cycles identified two core issues: (1) the local radial profile α(R) is asserted without any governing field equation, Lagrangian, or screening law — making the mechanism an adjustable ansatz rather than a prediction; and (2) the fitting procedure is substantially circular — X(R) is extracted by numerical differentiation of the observed velocity V_o, and the resulting baryonic curve V_bX is integrated back from the same data and compared against SPARC baryonic estimates, so agreement is not an independent test. A third major objection, elevated to "fatal" by one reviewer, is that the modified Friedmann equations assume a homogeneous, isotropic FLRW metric and cannot be applied as local gravitational source terms in a virialized disk galaxy without a derivation of the CCC post-Newtonian or thin-disk limit that is absent here. Quantitative goodness-of-fit metrics are absent; only visual agreement is reported for seven galaxies under a spherical approximation.
